justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), delivered-order rejection (AC-3), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-4), unauthorized user (AC-5), in-transit (AC-6), and more. At least 6 explicit ambiguities surfaced across escalations and low_confidence_areas (cancellation window policy, IN_TRANSIT handling, multi-package behavior, fee structure, notification requirements, who can cancel). confidence.level is 42, well below the 65 threshold. Business rules are clearly marked as invariant or policy with justifications, and assumptions are explicitly listed rather than presented as facts. edge_cases section is richly populated with 8 scenarios.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, authorization, validation, and event structure
confidence.level of 42 correctly reflects the severely underspecified input
5 detailed escalations with options and recommendations — surfaces cancellation window, IN_TRANSIT policy, multi-package ambiguity, fee structure, and notification requirements
Business rules explicitly distinguish invariants from policy decisions, avoiding presenting invented rules as facts
BR-2 and AC-5 address the 'who can cancel' question, defining it as an invariant while the escalations leave room for admin/ops cancellation as a separate concern
Edge cases section covers concurrency, payment failures, bulk operations, partial fulfillment, and eventual consistency — demonstrates deep domain thinking
Assumptions section transparently lists all inferred domain knowledge, making it easy for a PM to confirm or correct

BR-2 asserts 'only the requesting user may cancel' as an invariant without flagging that operations/support staff cancellation is an open question — this is partially mitigated by the confidence.assumptions noting 'user-initiated cancellation, not admin or system cancellation'

Cancellation fee rules (BR-3, BR-4) are presented with specific behavior (zero fee for PLACED/CONFIRMED, fee for ASSIGNED/PICKED_UP) which is reasonable but could be seen as inventing policy — mitigated by marking them as invariant:false and listing fee structure as an escalation

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well exceeding the 3 minimum) covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2, AC-3), already-delivered rejection (AC-4), already-cancelled rejection (AC-5), plus authorization, validation, concurrency, and notification edge cases. At least 5 explicit ambiguities surfaced across low_confidence_areas and escalations (cancellation fee structure, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY policy, partial cancellation, special item types, cancellation reason requirement, who can cancel). Confidence level is 40, well below the 65 threshold. Business rules that go beyond the spec (e.g., fee tiers, cancellation reason requirement) are clearly marked as non-invariant assumptions, not presented as facts. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceed the 3 minimum, covering happy path, both required edge cases (delivered and already-cancelled), plus authorization, validation, concurrency, and more
Confidence level of 40 appropriately reflects the underspecified single-sentence PM input
4 formal escalations with options and recommendations — ambiguities are not just listed but structured for PM decision-making
Business rules clearly distinguish invariants from policy decisions (invariant: true/false with justification), avoiding presenting invented rules as facts
Low confidence areas explicitly call out fee structure, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY policy, partial cancellation, special items, and cancellation reason — all genuine gaps in the spec
Edge cases section covers 8 scenarios including race conditions, refund failures, perishable items, and network partitions
Assumptions section transparently lists what Bird assumed (e.g., full order lifecycle, payment model, reason requirement) so stakeholders can validate

BR-4 and BR-5 define specific fee/refund policies (full refund before assignment, cancellation fee after assignment) that go slightly beyond flagging — though they are marked as non-invariant, the acceptance criteria (AC-1, AC-2) encode them as expected behavior rather than conditional on PM clarification
The 'Cancellation Window' term in ubiquitous language could be seen as inventing a concept not in the original spec, though it is a reasonable domain modeling term

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 8 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria (well beyond the 3 minimum), covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), non-cancellable states (AC-3), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-5), authorization (AC-4), concurrency (AC-8), and ambiguous states (AC-7). At least 5 explicit ambiguities surfaced in low_confidence_areas and 3 formal escalations covering cancellation window, who can cancel, and partial cancellation. Confidence level is 40, well below the 65 threshold. No business rules are presented as facts without caveats — assumptions are clearly labeled, and the escalations offer options rather than dictating answers. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 8 scenarios.

observations.text: Exceptional coverage: 8 acceptance criteria far exceed the 3 minimum, covering happy path, edge cases, concurrency, and authorization
Confidence level of 40 correctly reflects the underspecified input — well below the 65 threshold
Three formal escalations with options and recommendations address all key ambiguities: cancellation window states, who can cancel, and multi-package behavior
Explicitly flags 'Assigned' state as ambiguous rather than silently assuming a rule — dedicates an entire escalation to it
Does not invent a time-based cancellation window (e.g., '30 minutes') — correctly uses state-based boundaries and flags assumptions
Assumptions section clearly separates what Bird assumed from what was specified, making it easy for stakeholders to validate
Edge cases section is rich with 8 scenarios including race conditions, partial fulfillment, and payment edge cases

BR-2 states cancellation window is 'Placed or Confirmed' as an invariant rule, but then ESC-1 flags the Assigned state as ambiguous — slight tension between presenting it as invariant while also escalating it. Could be clearer that the invariant is contingent on escalation resolution

Cancellation fee details in BR-4 and BR-5 go beyond the spec without always being flagged inline as assumptions, though they are marked invariant:false and the confidence section does list fee structure as low-confidence
